coredata.dc:description: Timely healing of acute wounds and stopping wound chronicity are current and future priorities in wound therapy. It is urgent and relevant to develop a wound dressing that has antimicrobial and monitors the wound microenvironment in real time. In this study, quaternary ammonium chitosan (HTCC) was selected as the antimicrobial agent and CS/PEO/HTCC nanofiber membranes (CPHs) were prepared by electrostatic spinning technique. The nanofiber membrane (CPH91) with the best antimicrobial performance was screened by the disk diffusion method and drug susceptibility testing by dilution method, and its antimicrobial effect on S. aureus was better than that of E. coli. Subsequently, functional carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by solvothermal method and doped into CPH91 nanofibers by electrospinning. A good linear relationship between pH value (5.0–8.0) and the fluorescence intensity of CDs was observed. In addition, the nanofibers (CPH91@CDs) had good morphology, hydrophilicity, and biocompatibility. Changes in fluorescence intensity of CPH91@CDs at different pH (5.0–8.0) were monitored and converted into RGB values that were linearly fitted to pH value. Finally, the potential of CPH91@CDs of improving wound healing and instantaneously controlling wound healing process was confirmed by an infected wound model (S. aureus) on the back of SD rats.

head.ce:title: Smart chitosan-based nanofibers for real-time monitoring and promotion of wound healing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Timely healing of acute wounds and stopping wound chronicity are current and future priorities in wound therapy. It is urgent and relevant to develop a wound dressing that has antimicrobial and monitors the wound microenvironment in real time. In this study, quaternary ammonium chitosan (HTCC) was selected as the antimicrobial agent and CS/PEO/HTCC nanofiber membranes (CPHs) were prepared by electrostatic spinning technique. The nanofiber membrane (CPH91) with the best antimicrobial performance was screened by the disk diffusion method and drug susceptibility testing by dilution method, and its antimicrobial effect on S. aureus was better than that of E. coli. Subsequently, functional carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by solvothermal method and doped into CPH91 nanofibers by electrospinning. A good linear relationship between pH value (5.0–8.0) and the fluorescence intensity of CDs was observed. In addition, the nanofibers (CPH91@CDs) had good morphology, hydrophilicity, and biocompatibility. Changes in fluorescence intensity of CPH91@CDs at different pH (5.0–8.0) were monitored and converted into RGB values that were linearly fitted to pH value. Finally, the potential of CPH91@CDs of improving wound healing and instantaneously controlling wound healing process was confirmed by an infected wound model (S. aureus) on the back of SD rats.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Skin is the largest organ and outermost layer of defense of the human body, and its primary function is to prevent dehydration of the organism and to act as a physical/chemical barrier to protect the body from pathogens [1]. When the skin is damaged due to trauma or other reasons, a number of cascading reactions such as excessive secretion production, immune system disorders and bacterial infections are induced [2]. Wound types are classified as acute and chronic wounds based on healing [3]. Acute wounds are characterized by sudden formation and rapid healing, such as those resulting from abrasions. In comparison, chronic wounds are characterized by persistent inflammation, with a healing cycle generally exceeding 12 weeks, which can lead to ulceration and severe physiological contamination [4]. Therefore, it is important to develop functional wound dressings to facilitate timely repair of acute wounds and to prevent them from becoming chronic. Traditional medical dressings, such as cotton gauze and bandages, which merely provide physical isolation, are susceptible to bacterial growth, and are prone to form tissue adhesions when in prolonged contact with the wound. To overcome these drawbacks, many novel wound dressings, such as nanofibers [5,6], sponges [7] and hydrogels [8–10] have been designed and developed. Nanofibers with controlled pore structure and continuous homogeneity produced by electrostatic spinning technology are similar with the extracellular matrix of human, which facilitates cell adhesion and proliferation [11,12]. Porous properties of nanofibers lead to high permeability, supporting a wet environment for wound healing and providing excellent exudate absorption properties. Additionally, the large surface area improves loading efficiency of drug molecules [13,14]. It is essential for these benefits to improve wound healing and make damaged skin regenerate.

Chitosan (CS) is the product of deacetylation of the natural polysaccharide chitin [15]. Chitosan-based nanofibers have been widely used in biomedical applications for good biocompatibility, tissue adhesion and inherent antimicrobial and hemostatic properties [16,17]. However, the dissolution of chitosan has deeply influenced its antimicrobial activity, and its use alone cannot satisfy the needs of wound dressing applications. Its antimicrobial activity can be enhanced by chemical modification or combination with other antimicrobial materials [18–20]. Quaternary ammonium chitosan (HTCC) is a derivative of chitosan, which has properties such as moisturizing, easily soluble in water and antibacterial [21]. The quaternary ammonium group (-NR3+) in its structure carries stronger charges than the amino group (−NH2) alone in chitosan, and the high surface charge is prone to have a destructive effect on the bacterial cell wall [22]. HTCC can be used in the biomedical field as scar and trauma repair [23], anti-infection [21,24,25] and other product applications. Therefore, we propose to design CS/HTCC nanofibers that combine their synergistic advantages, including the unique biological properties of chitosan, the structural advantages of nanofiber membranes, and the excellent antibacterial properties of HTCC.

Early detection of infected or non-healing wounds will facilitate targeted treatments and prevent chronicity of the wound. The key factors affecting wound healing are temperature, oxygen levels, enzymes and pH. Based on these factors, it is important to monitor pH at different stages during wound healing process. Generally, healthy skin has a pH value ranges from 4.0 to 6.0, whereas after injury or infection, the pH of wound site is clearly increased (7.0–9.0) [26]. When an acute wound becomes infected, there is a significant increase in secretions as well as inflammatory cells around it, and the wound is subsequently alkaline. After administration of medication or debridement of the wound, the acutely infected wound returns to the normal healing process [27–30]. Most of the chronic wounds are also in an infected state with a pH range of 6.5–8.5 (mean at 7.42) [31]. Timely healing of acute wounds and preventing them from becoming chronic is the focus of current as well as future wound treatment. Therefore, the development of wound dressings that are both bacteriostatic and able to detect the wound microenvironments is particularly urgent. Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials that have attracted widespread attention for their applications in bioimaging, drug delivery, biosensors and gene delivery due to their fluorescence stability, low toxicity and biocompatibility [32–36]. Studies have shown that changing pH value can affect the optical properties of fluorescent CDs [35,37]. CDs is highly sensitive to pH, which may be related to the different surface states, various functional groups and energy gaps [38–41]. It is found that CDs can be considered as a viable tool for monitoring wound healing [42]. CDs also have good antimicrobial properties. The antimicrobial mechanisms of CDs include physical disruption of bacterial surfaces, generation of ROS (reactive oxygen species) to disrupt bacterial structures, interference with the structure of bacterial DNA and RNA, coupling with antibiotics, disruption of bacterial peptidoglycan biosynthesis, and inducing programmed bacterial death [34,43–45].

In the present study, we prepared a series of CS/PEO/HTCC nanofiber membranes (CPHs) by selecting HTCC as the antimicrobial agent and using electrostatic spinning technology (Scheme 1 ). The antimicrobial performance of CPHs doped with different mass concentrations of HTCC was surveyed by disk diffusion method and drug susceptibility testing by dilution method on E. coli (ATCC 8739) and S. aureus (ATCC 6538). After screening CPHs with best antibacterial effect, the functional carbon dots doped CPH nanofiber membranes (CPH@CDs) were prepared by electrostatic spinning. Conventional physical characterization of carbon dots and CPH@CDs was performed. A clear relationship between pH and the fluorescence intensity of the nanofibers was established by a smart device. In addition, the biocompatibility of CPH@CDs nanofiber membranes was assessed by in vitro hemolysis and cytotoxicity tests. Ultimately, the potential of CPH@CDs of promoting wound healing was assessed by means of an infected wound model (S. aureus) on SD rats and changes in the fluorescence intensity of the dressing were recorded.

CS (viscosity of 100–200 mPa·s, deacetylation degree ≥95 %) and polyethylene oxide (average Mv ≈ 600,000) were purchased from Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Quaternary ammonium chitosan was obtained from Zhejiang Aoxing Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Zhejiang, China). D‑sodium gluconate, ammonium persulfate, ethylenediamine and diethylenetriamine were acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. E. coli (ATCC 8739) and S. aureus (ATCC 6538) were purchased from Beijing Microbiological Culture Collection Center (Beijing, China), Columbia Blood Agar (OK3001) were purchased from Wuhu Ouke Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuhu, China), PPLO Broth Base (AC12667-250 g) were purchased from Shanghai Acmec Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The carbon dots were prepared by the solvothermal method. Firstly, 0.5 g of ammonium persulfate, 0.5 g of D‑sodium gluconate and 1.0 mL of ethylenediamine were mixed at room temperature with stirring until a pale-yellow viscous consistency was achieved, then added 10 mL of diethylenetriamine, and remained stirring for 2–3 h until the mixture became a pale-yellow opalescence. The solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reactor (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. During the reaction, the mixture darkened to an orange-yellow color. Next, the above solution using a dialysis bag (MWCO: 8–10 KD) in pure water was dialyzed for 3 days (change water every 12 h). Finally, the purified carbon dots were evenly dispersed in pure water and stored at 4 °C.

CS and PEO powders (mCS: mPEO= 75: 25) were co-dissolved in 90 % acetic acid and stirred for half day at room temperature to obtain CS/PEO solution (4 %, w/w). The HTCC solution (6 wt%) was prepared and mixed with chitosan/PEO solution, which was continued to stir for 1 h at 25 °C to obtain a homogeneous CS/PEO-HTCC solution. The mixed solution was filled a 5 mL syringe and the distance between the syringe needle and the collector was adjusted to 18 cm. The machine parameters were set to a positive voltage of 15–17 kV and a push rate of 0.080 mm/min. The experimental operation was carried out under ambient temperature of 25 °C and the humidity of 30 %. After collecting, the nanofiber membranes were vacuum dried at 60 °C for one day to obtain the final samples. The fibrous films were named CPH0, CPH91 and CPH82 based on the mass ratio of CS/PEO solution to HTCC solution (10: 0, 9: 1 and 8: 2).

The 0.5 mL CDs solution (6 mg/mL) was added into 5 mL CPH91 solution and stirred for 1 h to obtain a pre-spinning solution. Spinning was carried out under the conditions of positive voltage of 15.5–16 kV, receiving distance of 19 cm and maintain push-injection speed at 0.080 mm/min. The ambient temperature was 25 °C and the humidity was controlled at about 35 %. After electrospinning, the CPH@CDs nanofiber membrane on aluminium foil was vacuum dried at 60 °C for one day.

The samples were prepared by dropwise addition of aqueous carbon dots onto copper meshes, dried with an infrared lamp and then used transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200S, USA) to observe microstructures of samples. The particle size distributions were counted using Image J and Origin software.

Fluorescence intensity was measured by adding 100 μL of CDs solution in phosphate buffered saline solution (PBS) of different pH (5.0–8.0), mixing well and transferring to a fluorescence cuvette using a type fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, UK). The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs were recorded between 380–600 nm under excitation at 365 nm.

The chemical composition of the CDs power was determined using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 380, USA). The infrared spectra of CDs powders were using the potassium bromide (KBr) compression test method with a wave number range of 4000–400 cm−1 and a scan number of 64 with a resolution of 4 cm−1.
The Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer (Japan) was utilized to analyze the X-ray diffraction (XRD) of CDs under the the scanning range of 10° ∼ 80°.

The fiber morphology of CPH91 and CPH91@CDs at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV was observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, TESCAN MIRA LMS, Czech Republic) at magnifications of 3000 × and 5000 ×. The distribution of fiber diameters and arithmetic mean were measured and analyzed by using Image J and Origin software. In addition, the scanning electron microscope image of CPH91@CDs with a magnification of 3000 × were selected for Mapping scanning and the distribution of C, O, N, and S elements were detected.
Dynamic water contact angles of CPH91 and CPH91@CDs were measured on a contact angle meter (Chengde Dingsheng JY-82C, China) and sessile drop technique.

Samples were cut into long strips (50 mm in length × 10 mm in width). On a universal testing machine (MTS C42.503, USA), mechanical properties of CPH91 and CPH91@CDs were tested for three times at a crosshead speed of 10 mm/min.

PBS solution (1 mL) of different pH values (5.0–8.0) was taken and added dropwise onto the surface of CPH91@CDs nanofiber membrane (1 × 1 cm) to completely infiltrate it. The treated samples were placed in a UV instrument (WD-9403F, China), and the changes in fluorescence intensity were observed under UV light (365 nm) and photographed for recording.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial evaluation of CPHs nanofiber membranes
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli (ATCC 8739) and S. aureus (ATCC 6538) were selected as bacterial models, and antimicrobial properties of CPH0, CPH91 and CPH82 were evaluated by the disk diffusion method. The drug susceptibility was tested by dilution method on bacteria.

The Columbia Blood Agar (OK3001) were purchased from Wuhu Ouke Biotechnology Co., Ltd. as a ready-to-use medium. Removed the medium and rewarmed to room temperature, a total of 100 μL of bacterial solution (108 CFU/mL) was evenly spread on the solid medium. UV-sterilized discs of the three samples with a diameter of 6 mm were pasted onto the medium, and gently pressed with sterile tweezers in order to make the samples in full contact with the medium. The samples were incubated at 37 °C for one day. Photographs were taken to observe the bacterial growth at the bottom and around the samples, and the diameter of the ring of inhibition was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug susceptibility testing by dilution method
ce:section.ce:para: Each sterile tube was filled with 2 mL bacterial solution at 1 × 108/mL concentration, followed by UV-sterilized samples of various concentrations (2 mg, 4 mg, 8 mg, 16 mg, 32 mg and 64 mg) were added to the corresponding tubes. Negative control tubes were added with culture medium only and positive control tubes were added with bacterial solution only. These tubes were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h with shaking, and the turbidity of the bacterial solution in each tube was observed after the incubation was completed. The OD value of 200 μL per well was measured at 625 nm using a microplate reader (BIO-TEK, USA). The OD values were used to determine the MIC50 and MIC90 of each sample and to calculate the antibacterial rate (AR), MIC50 and MIC90 refer to the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) required to inhibit the growth of 50 % or 90 % of the tested bacteria in the experiment, respectively.

Hemolysis assay was used to assess the hemocompatibility of CPH91 and CPH91@CDs nanofiber membranes. Anticoagulated blood was centrifuged to get erythrocytes. Then the erythrocytes and PBS solution (1:9, v/v) were mixed to obtain an erythrocyte suspension. Experimental group was the mixture (1 mg/mL, 2 mg/mL and 5 mg/mL) of sample and erythrocyte suspension. Positive control group was the mixture of erythrocytes and deionized water (1: 9, v/v) and the negative control groups was the mixture of erythrocyte suspension without the sample. All groups were incubated at 37 °C for 1 h and then centrifuged at 2000 g for 15 min. The supernatant absorbance was measured at 540 nm and the hemolysis rate was calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)

L929 mouse fibroblast cells suspension (100 μL per well) was inoculated in 96-well plates and cultured according to the experiment requirements. The nanofibre membranes were prepared as a square sheet with a side length of 1 cm, UV sterilized and co-cultured with cells for 24 h. The L929 cell viability was measured using the CCK-8 kit. Normal control groups were the wells, which was added with cell culture medium. And the solvent groups were those without added cells. All groups were incubated in the cell incubator for 2 h and then measured the absorbance values at 450 nm. Assessment of whether CNs are toxic to the cells was measured by cell viability at each time point (Eq. (2)).
(2)

Cells were co-cultured with samples for 1 day and the culture solution was aspirated. The cells were washed by PBS, to which 250 μL Calcein AM/PI assay solution was added, and incubated at 37 °C for half an hour in dark. The staining effect was observed under a fluorescence microscope (green fluorescence for Calcein AM, Ex/Em = 494/517 nm; red fluorescence for PI, Ex/Em = 535/617 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of wound healing in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Twelve male SD rats (6–8 weeks, 180–200 g) were equally divided into three groups. After dehairing anesthesia (10 % chloral hydrate), two total defect wounds of 0.8 cm diameter were created on the back of each rat. After simple hemostatic treatment, 0.06 mL of S.aureus (ATCC 6538, 106 CFU/mL) bacterial solution was added to the wounds and wounds were covered by the experimental group (CPH91, CPH91@CDs) and control group (sterile gauze) and fixed with an elastic bandage after 2 min. All samples were sterilized by UV radiation. Photographs were taken and areas measured on days 0, 3, 7, 10 and 14, respectively. In addition, changes in the fluorescence intensity of the dressings were photographed and recorded. The animal experiment was complied with the National Research Council's Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals and was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Yi Shengyuan Gene Technology (Tianjin) Co., Ltd. (Approval numbers YSY-DWLL-2023136).

Each experiment was repeated at least three times and data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Multiple t-test was used to determine the significant differences. Significant differences were defined as *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001.

The CDs morphological structure was characterized by transmission electron microscopy. In Fig. 1a, the CDs had a nearly spherical structure and were well dispersed in aqueous medium. Their diameters ranged from 7 to 13 nm and were concentrated around 10 nm (Fig. 1b). Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs were measured in PBS buffer solution at pH 5.0–8.0. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence was observed in Fig. 1c. Meanwhile, the CDs fluorescence intensity was more significantly affected by pH values. As shown in Fig. 1d, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs significantly decreased with increasing pH values from 5.0 to 8.0. The mechanism of the change of fluorescence CDs with pH might be the deprotonation of the CDs surface group [46]. The surface of CDs was rich in functional groups as shown in Fig. 1e. CDs contained functional groups such as hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl, carbon‑nitrogen bond and carbonyl groups. There was a distinct absorption peak at 3442 cm−1, which was the result of NH or OH stretching vibration. In addition, absorption peaks representing CH, CO, CC and -COO at 2920 cm−1, 1647 cm−1, 1556 cm−1,1383 cm−1 were also observed, respectively. These functional groups had good hydrophilicity, which made CDs have good water solubility [44,47,48]. The x-ray diffractogram (XRD) of CDs was shown in Fig. 1f. The XRD image showed a diffraction peak approximately at 2θ = 21.2°, which corresponded to the (002) crystal plane diffraction of graphite [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameters of CPHs nanofiber membrane preparation and hydrophilicity of CPH91 and CPH91@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: According to the component ratios in Fig. 2a 5 mL of spinning solution was prepared, and 0.5 mL CDs solution was added. The data related to the electrostatic spinning machine (ET-2535H, Beijing) were set according to the machine parameters in Fig. 2b. From Fig. 2b, it was found that with the addition of CDs the positive voltage strengthened and the push injection speed decreased. It might be due to the fact that the introduction of CDs into the mixture solution increases the gravitational force and the viscosity of the spinning solution. The spun out samples were subjected to antimicrobial reality, as detailed in Section 3.4. According to the experiments,
CPH91 and CPH91@CDs showed excellent antimicrobial properties, and the following experiments would explore the relevant properties of CPH91 and CPH@CDs.

The hydrophilicity of CPH91 and CPH91@CDs was assessed by measuring the change in dynamic water contact angle within 60 s (Fig. 2c). Because of hydrophilic groups on the surface of chitosan and the excellent water solubility of PEO and quaternary ammonium chitosan, CPH91 exhibited well hydrophilicity. The water contact could be seen that angle decreased dramatically within 30 s and the water droplets were almost completely absorbed by sample. Compared with CPH91, the surface wettability of CPH91@CDs after doping with carbon dots was average, but it also had good hydrophilicity, and its contact angle decreased from 104.55° to 59.8° in 30 s. This hydrophilic dressing facilitates the absorption of wound exudates as well as promotes the fibroblast adhesion and proliferation, thus accelerating wound healing [50,51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM, diameter distribution and elements analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM images of CPH91 and CPH91@CDs nanofiber membranes were shown in Fig. 3a and b, respectively. The fiber morphology of both CPH91 and CPH91@CDs was good, with the fibers being continuous, homogeneous and free of beading. As shown in Fig. 3c and d for the diameter distribution of two types of nanofibers, the average fiber diameters of CPH91 and CPH91@CDs were 272.62 ± 70.27 nm and 189.51 ± 38.77 nm, respectively. Addition of carbon point aqueous solution decreased the diameter of the fibers obtained after spinning, which may be related to the further reduction of the viscosity of the spinning system. At this time, the mobility of the polymer chains during spinning was high and unstable, and the jets were easily drawn under electric field force, so the diameter of fibers decreased [52].

Mapping images of different elements in CPH91@CDs were shown in Fig. 3e, where element S was the characteristic element of carbon dots, it can be seen that element S was more uniformly distributed in the fibers, which also indicated that the carbon dots were well dispersed in the pre-spinning solution.

Ideal wound dressing could provide adequate mechanical support, and a certain degree of flexibility [17]. Tensile tests were performed on CPH91 and CPH91@CDs nanofiber membranes and the results were shown in Fig. 3f. Tensile strength of CPH91@CDs (12.61 ± 0.56 MPa) was slightly higher than that of CPH91 (12.26 ± 0.69 MPa). It has been reported in the literature that adding nanoparticles to polymer matrix improves tensile strength of materials, depending on the volume, size and mass of the particles [53–56]. The elongation at break of CPH91 and CPH91@CDs were 17.44 ± 1.82 % and 12.85 ± 1.13 %, respectively, which indicated that the carbon dots had an effect on the flexibility of the nanofiber membranes.

The pH value is a very important monitoring index in the process of wound healing, which can reflect both the wound infection and the reconstruction process [57]. We designed a wound healing monitoring system with varying fluorescence intensity with pH-responsive CDs.

Considering the applicability of such dressing for real-time monitoring of wound status in a clinical application scenarios, it is a need to establish a quantitative relationship between pH and this fluorescence change. Some of the current methods such as color differentiation under visual inspection and electrical signal measurement are problematic. Visual evaluation of color discrimination is prone to errors, while electrodes are susceptible to contamination, resulting in inaccurate signals [58]. To overcome the detection limitations of traditional methods, the images can be converted to remote RGB detection mode and regressively analyzed between the RGB values of the images and the pH values. Ultimately, a smart device (which analyzes the RGB values of the image) and a fitted equation can be used to obtain a more accurate wound pH value [42]. Under UV irradiation (365 nm), the G, B, and R values of CPH91@CDs nanofiber membranes all decreased gradually with increasing pH, with G and B values correlating with pH to a higher extent (Fig. 3g). The linear regression Eq. (3) was as follows:
(3)
pH = 9.36 −
G + B
65.04

The loading of carbon dots onto fibers using electrostatic spinning technique can extend the service life and application range of CDs. In Fig. 3h, the variation of fluorescence intensity was observed for CPH91@CDs nanofiber membrane loaded with CDs at different pH values. Under 365 nm excitation, fluorescence intensity of nanofiber membranes gradually diminished with increasing pH, and this change was most pronounced between pH 6.5 and 7.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial evaluation of CPHs nanofiber membranes
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4a for the results of the disk diffusion test of CPHs on E. coli ATCC 8739) and S. aureus (ATCC 6538), respectively, it can be clearly seen that the three samples have a poor antibacterial effect on E. coli, and there was no zone of inhibition appearing around the samples while there was a certain number of colonies at the bottom. In addition, it can be observed that CPH0 has basically no antibacterial effect on S. aureus. In contrast, the diameter of the ring of inhibition was about 6 mm. There were almost no colonies at the bottom of CPH91 and CPH82 samples, which could indicate that CPHs doped with quaternary ammonium chitosan had a certain antibacterial effect on S. aureus according to the GB/T 20944.1–2007 standard (agar flat dish diffusion method).

To further quantify the antibacterial results of CPHs nanofiber membranes against the two bacteria, the drug susceptibility testing by dilution method was used to assess the antibacterial performance of CPH0, CPH91 and CPH82 and the results were shown in Fig. 4b and c. The MIC50 values of CPH0 against E. coli and S. aureus were both above 32 mg/mL, which could indicate a poorer antibacterial effect of CPH0, consistent with the results of the disk diffusion experiments. The MIC50 of CPH82 against E. coli was 32 mg/mL, while that of CPH91 was only 2 mg/mL. However, when the concentration of CPH91 reached 32 mg/mL, the antibacterial rate was still <60 %, indicating that the inhibition of CPHs against E. coli was indeed poor. The MIC50 of CPH82 and CPH91 against S. aureus were both 1 mg/mL, but there was a difference in their antibacterial rates. The antibacterial rate of CPH91 against S. aureus was around 80 % at a concentration of 32 mg/mL, whereas the antibacterial rate of CPH82 was <70 % at the same sample concentration. Furthermore, in the preparation process, due to the excellent water solubility of quaternary ammonium chitosan and the amount of quaternary ammonium chitosan in CPH82 was more than that in CPH91, which would make the viscosity of the spinning liquid system of CPH82 decrease, making the positive voltage in the process of electrostatic spinning need to be adjusted to 17–18 kV to sustain the spinning process, which was a certain amount of consumption and damage to the equipment. In summary, the CPH91 spinning liquid system was selected to dope carbon dots in the subsequent experiments.

The hemolysis rate is an important indicator of blood compatibility. As shown in Fig. 5a, the hemolysis rates of both CPH91 and CPH91@CDs can be seen that were around 1 %, which was within the safety limits of biomedical material applications (<5 % hemolysis rate) [59]. This indicated that the hemocompatibility of the two nanofiber membranes was good and that there was basically no risk of blood cells rupture in contact with them. As the concentration of the sample increased, the hemolysis rate also increased. The hemolysis rate of CPH91 and CPH91@CD at 2 mg/mL were <5 %, which was within the safe range. However, when the concentration of CPH91 was increased to 5 mg/mL, the hemolysis rate was >5 %. The hemolysis rate increased with increasing nanofiber membrane concentration. Meanwhile, it can also be seen in Fig. 5a that there was a large amount of sample floating in the supernatant, which would not be used in practical applications to reach this concentration dosage of 5 mg/mL.

As shown in Fig. 5c, the results that the effects of CPH91 and CPH91@CDs on L929 mouse fibroblasts were detected by CCK-8 assay. As seen, the cell survival rate of both groups of samples after 24 h of co-culture with the cells was around 95 % and no significant difference between groups. Meanwhile, results of staining of cells after co-culture with the samples using Calcein/PI fluorescent dye were shown in Fig. 5c, and it was found that the L929 cells were all predominantly viable (green fluorescence). In conclusion, it can be concluded that both CPH91 and CPH91@CDs nanofiber membranes have low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of wound healing in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Pro-wound healing ability of CPH91 and CPH91@CDs was evaluated by establishing an infected wound model (S. aureus) on the back of SD rats. The process of wound healing after treatment with the three groups of samples (the gauze group was the control group) on days 0, 3, 7, 10, and 14 was shown in Fig. 6a. At each time point, the images of the wounds showed that the treated wound had a smaller area of the nanofiber membranes than the control. The wound contraction rates after different dressing treatments at different times were shown in Fig. 6b.

The good pro-wound healing ability of CPH91 and CPH91@CDs started to show from day 3, and their wound contraction rates were both close to 100 % at day 14. It can be seen that on days 3 and 7, the wound healing of CPH91 and CPH91@CDs had a significant advantage over the gauze group. It was clear to see on day 3 that the pro-wound healing rate of CPH91@CDs was comparable to that of CPH91 and on day 7 the wound healing rate of CPH91@CDs was even slightly better than that of CPH91, which further indicated that CDs have good biocompatibility. This is not only due to the biological properties of chitosan and quaternary ammonium chitosan, but also to the structure of the nanofibers prepared by the electrostatic spinning technique. This three-dimensional network structure similar to the human extracellular matrix, facilitates cell adhesion, proliferation and growth, and also promotes tissue regeneration.

Additionally, it can be observed that there was a more significant change in the fluorescence intensity of the dressing between days 3–10 as shown in Fig. 6c. The RGB values of the images as well as the linear eq. (3) gave the approximate wound pH ranges of 7.0–8.0, 6.5–7.0, and 5.5–6.0 at days 3, 7 and 10. When acute wounds become infected, there was a marked increase in wound secretions and inflammatory cells, and the wounds appeared to have a markedly alkaline pH value. After dressing and debridement, the secretions and bacteria from the wound were removed, and the wound will return to the normal healing process of the acute wound. During wound healing, the pH value of the wound gradually changed to an acidic value. Acidic pH can inhibit bacterial growth, reduce protease activity and promote the release of more oxygen, which is an important sign of wound healing [60]. The change in wound pH also demonstrated the ability of the CPH91@CDs nanofiber membrane to heal acutely infected wounds in a timely manner, thereby preventing the wounds from going chronic. All in all, it is clear that CPH91 and CPH91@CDs have good antimicrobial effects and are beneficial and effective in accelerating wound healing.

In this research, HTCC was selected as the antimicrobial agent and doped into CS/PEO using electrostatic spinning technique to prepare CPHs nanofibers with different mass ratios of HTCC. The CPH91, a nanofiber membrane with the best antimicrobial properties, was screened by the disk diffusion testing and the drug susceptibility testing by dilution method, and its antibacterial effect against S. aureus were better than that of E. coli. In addition, CDs were synthesized by solvothermal method and doped into CPH91 nanofibers by electrospinning. Fluorescence intensity of these functional CDs was significantly affected by pH, and decreased significantly with increasing pH values. This CDs-doped nanofiber (CPH91@CDs) has good morphology, hydrophilicity, and biocompatibility. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity changes of CPH91@CDs under different pH values (5.0–8.0) were recorded and converted into RGB values by a smart device, and a linear relationship was established between the RGB values and pH values. Finally, the potential of this dressing for antibacterial efficacy, wound healing promotion and real-time monitoring in wound healing was also demonstrated by the infected wound models (S. aureus) on the back of SD rats.

Therefore, CPH91@CDs CPH91@CD has the potential to be used as a wound dressing for effective measurement of microenvironment monitoring and wound healing. CPH91@CDs has antimicrobial and wound healing properties, as well as good biocompatibility and hematocompatibility. CPH91@CDs also has a good potential to be used as a wound dressing to observe changes in pH during the wound healing process in real time without the need to remove the dressing, which would not result in secondary damage to the wound. The focus of this study was on the preparation and evaluation of dressings for real-time monitoring of wound healing, and therefore the multifunctional integration of the materials will be intensively investigated in the future.
